_meta.name: Wild Corpus — realistic user-style queries Layer 0/1 was NOT optimized for
_meta.purpose: Adversarial / red-team testing. Each query simulates how real users actually communicate with chat AIs, including patterns the engineer (me) did not specifically design for. Failures here are honest bugs, not knowing-the-test-set artifacts.
_meta.ground_truth_policy: Best-effort — for some queries the 'correct' modality is genuinely ambiguous. Where ambiguous, accept multiple labels.

I'm trying to understand transformers. From what I've read, the key insight is the self-attention mechanism. It allows each position in a sequence to attend to all other positions, which is what makes them so powerful for language modeling. But I don't fully understand the math. The paper says Q, K, V matrices are computed by multiplying the input embeddings with learned weight matrices. Then attention scores are softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k))V. But why sqrt(d_k)? And why three separate matrices instead of one? Also confused about multi-head attention — what does 'multiple heads' actually mean? Can you walk me through this from scratch?

I've been working on this project for like three weeks now and I'm stuck on a really specific issue. So basically what's happening is when I deploy to staging the database migration runs fine but when I check the data afterward it looks like maybe half the rows are missing? I checked the migration script and it does have a WHERE clause that filters by tenant_id but the test database has multiple tenants so I'm not sure if that's causing issues. The migration tool is alembic and we're on PostgreSQL 14. I also tried running the migration manually on a copy of the database and it worked fine there. So I'm thinking maybe there's a race condition or something with how alembic handles transactions but I'm not sure how to debug it. Any ideas?
